concepts.title: Animal Support Systems and Locomotion Overview
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the different types of support systems in animals, **explain** the structure and function of the human skeleton and joints, and **identify** common diseases related to the skeletal system, as per the Grade 10 CAPS curriculum for Animal Support Systems and Locomotion.

concepts.title: Types of Skeletons
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** and **give examples** of animals possessing a hydrostatic skeleton, an endoskeleton, and an exoskeleton. They must **compare** the **advantages and disadvantages** of each type and **explain** their developmental progression, relating it to the need for support in a terrestrial lifestyle.

concepts.title: Hydrostatic Skeleton
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the characteristics of a hydrostatic skeleton, **provide examples** of organisms possessing it, and **explain** its advantages and disadvantages.

concepts.title: Exoskeleton
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the characteristics of an exoskeleton, **provide examples** of organisms possessing it, and **explain** its advantages and disadvantages. Visual identification may be required through observation of models or photographs.

concepts.title: Endoskeleton
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the characteristics of an endoskeleton, **provide examples** of organisms possessing it, and **explain** its advantages and disadvantages.

concepts.title: Human Skeleton: Structure and Function
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **identify** and **describe** the components of the human skeleton, differentiating between the axial and appendicular skeletons. They must also **describe** the main **functions** of the skeleton including movement, protection, support, mineral storage, and hearing. Visual identification/drawing required for a typical long bone and its longitudinal section, and observation of models or photographs.

concepts.title: Axial Skeleton
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **identify** and **mention** the components of the axial skeleton, including facial bones, cranium, foramen magnum, palate, and jaws, linking its structure to human evolution (Grade 12). Visual identification/drawing required through observation of models or photographs.

concepts.title: Appendicular Skeleton
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **identify** and **describe** the components of the appendicular skeleton. Visual identification/drawing required through observation of models or photographs.

concepts.title: Functions of the Human Skeleton
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **explain** and **list** the key functions of the human skeleton, including its roles in movement, protection, support, storage of minerals, and hearing.

concepts.title: Structure of a Long Bone
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the structure of a typical long bone and **explain** the relationship between the structure and function of its associated tissues: bone, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments. They must be able to **observe** and **draw** a typical long bone showing a longitudinal section, and **observe** these tissues using fresh material (e.g., from a butcher).

concepts.title: Types of Joints and Locomotion
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **classify** and **describe** the different types of joints in the human body: fixed, partly movable, and freely movable (synovial). They should **explain** the structure of various synovial joints (ball and socket, hinge, pivot, gliding) and **describe** the specific movements that occur at each type of joint. If possible, learners must **observe** an X-ray of ball and socket and hinge joints.

concepts.title: Fixed Joints
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **define** fixed joints and **provide examples** of their location in the human skeleton, such as those found in the skull.

concepts.title: Partly Movable Joints
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **define** partly movable joints and **provide examples** of their location in the human skeleton, such as those between vertebrae.

concepts.title: Freely Movable (Synovial) Joints
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the detailed structure of synovial joints, including ball and socket, hinge, pivot, and gliding joints. They must also **explain** the specific types of movement (e.g., flexion, extension, rotation) permitted by each of these freely movable joints. If possible, learners should **observe** X-rays or models of these joints.

concepts.title: Roles of Bones, Joints, and Muscles in Locomotion
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **explain** the coordinated roles of bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and antagonistic muscles (e.g., biceps/triceps) in facilitating human locomotion. They should also **describe** the structure of voluntary skeletal muscles, including myofibrils, and **outline** the basic mechanism of muscle contraction.

concepts.title: Components of Locomotion: Bones, Ligaments, Tendons
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the specific roles of bones, ligaments, and tendons in providing support and enabling movement in human locomotion.

concepts.title: Voluntary Skeletal Muscles and Contraction
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **describe** the structure of voluntary skeletal muscles, including myofibrils, and **explain** the basic mechanism of muscle contraction, particularly how antagonistic muscles (e.g., biceps and triceps) facilitate movement at joints.

concepts.title: Diseases Affecting the Skeletal System
concepts.body: Learners must be able to **identify** and **describe** common diseases and abnormalities that affect the human skeletal system, including rickets in children, osteoporosis, and arthritis, stating their causes, symptoms, and potential treatments.
